content:
# Chapter 6: No-Code and Low-Code Platforms

*In which we discover you can build surprisingly powerful things without writing much code at all.*

## The No-Code Revolution Is Real

There's a debate in developer communities about no-code and low-code tools. The traditionalists say real developers write real code. The pragmatists say: if it ships and works, who cares?

The pragmatists are right.

No-code and low-code tools have gotten genuinely impressive. For certain types of apps — especially internal tools, MVPs, workflows, and automations — you can build faster with these tools than with code. Not slightly faster. Dramatically faster.

That said, they have limits. Knowing those limits is what this chapter is about.

---

## What's the Difference?

**No-code:** Build entirely through visual interfaces. No programming syntax. Think drag-and-drop, form-fill configuration, point-and-click logic. Webflow, Bubble, Glide, Notion.

**Low-code:** Mostly visual, but with escape hatches for custom code when you need it. You might write a JavaScript function for custom logic but assemble the rest visually. Retool, Softr, Framer.

**Vibe-coded:** A newer category that barely existed when traditional no-code tools launched — AI generates the code for you from a description. Lovable, Bolt.new, Cursor, v0. You describe what you want; the AI writes the code; you review and tweak.

The line between all three is blurring fast.

---

## Category 1: Workflow Automation (Zapier, Make, n8n)

These tools connect apps together and automate workflows — the "if this, then that" of software.

**Zapier:** The most popular. 7,000+ integrations. You build "Zaps" — automated workflows triggered by events. "When a new row is added to this Google Sheet, summarize it with AI and send an email." No code. Zero.

**Make (formerly Integromat):** More powerful than Zapier, better for complex multi-step flows. Slightly steeper learning curve.

**n8n:** Open source, self-hostable, increasingly popular. More technical than Zapier but incredibly flexible.

**For AI apps:** These tools have built-in OpenAI and Anthropic integrations. You can build AI-powered workflows without a single line of code: classify incoming emails with AI, generate product descriptions from SKU data, auto-respond to customer inquiries, summarize meeting notes.

**Best for:** Business workflows, automations, integrating AI into existing tools, prototyping AI features before building them properly.

**Limits:** Not suitable for apps with custom UIs or complex user interactions. Gets expensive at scale (Zapier pricing can surprise you). Logic gets unwieldy for complex scenarios.

> 🐇 **Bunny Tip**
> Before writing any code for a workflow-type feature, ask yourself: can I build this in n8n or Make? You might save days of development. If it works, great. If you later need more control, you can rebuild it in code with a clear spec.

---

## Category 2: App Builders (Bubble, Glide, Softr)

These tools let you build full web or mobile apps visually.

**Bubble:** The most powerful no-code web app builder. Build real web apps with a database, user authentication, custom logic, and a custom UI — all without code. Has AI integrations via plugins. Complex to learn but genuinely powerful.

**Glide:** Turns Google Sheets or Airtable into mobile apps. Extremely fast for simple data-driven apps. Has built-in AI features. Limited flexibility compared to Bubble.

**Softr:** Similar to Glide. Turns Airtable into web apps. Good for client portals, directories, internal tools.

**For AI apps:** Bubble has OpenAI plugins that let you make API calls from your app logic. You can build an AI-powered app in Bubble — a form that sends input to GPT and displays the response — without touching code.

**Best for:** MVPs, internal tools, simple consumer apps, building fast to validate an idea.

**Limits:** Bubble is notoriously slow to load. Complex logic becomes hard to debug. Vendor lock-in is real — migrating off Bubble later is painful. Not suitable for high-performance production apps.

---

## Category 3: AI-Powered Code Generation (Lovable, Bolt, v0, Cursor)

This is the new category that's changing everything. You describe what you want; AI writes the code; you have a working app in minutes.

**Lovable (formerly GPT Engineer):** Describe your app in natural language; get a full-stack React + Supabase app. Genuinely impressive for MVPs.

**Bolt.new:** Similar concept from StackBlitz. Generates full-stack apps from prompts, runs them in the browser immediately.

**v0 (by Vercel):** Generates React UI components from descriptions. Perfect for building frontend pieces quickly.

**Cursor:** A code editor with an AI pair programmer built in. You write prompts, it writes code, you review. Not no-code — you still look at code — but dramatically accelerates coding.

**For AI apps:** These tools are already built for the AI era. They'll generate the OpenAI API calls, the frontend, the backend — you just describe the behavior you want.

**Best for:** MVPs, prototyping, learning by example, generating boilerplate, solo developers who want to move fast.

**Limits:** Generated code can be messy. You still need to understand code to review and debug it. Not suitable for production systems without significant engineering review.

---

## Category 4: AI-Native App Platforms (Flowise, Langflow, Dify)

These are visual builders specifically for AI workflows — connecting models, prompts, tools, and data sources.

**Flowise:** Visual drag-and-drop for building LLM chains and agents. Want a RAG pipeline? Drag in a "document loader," a "text splitter," an "embedding model," a "vector store," and a "chat model." Connect them. Done.

**Langflow:** Similar to Flowise, built on LangChain.

**Dify:** More polished, supports deployment as an app, has a marketplace of templates.

**Best for:** Prototyping AI pipelines without code, experimenting with different model configurations, building internal AI tools.

**Limits:** Less flexible than code for complex custom behavior. The visual approach breaks down for complex conditional logic.

---

## The No-Code to Code Spectrum

Think of it as a dial, not a binary choice:

```
Pure No-Code ◄──────────────────────────────► Pure Code
Zapier   Bubble   Lovable/Bolt   Cursor   Raw SDKs
```

Where you sit on the dial depends on:
- How custom is the behavior you need?
- How much scale will this reach?
- How much do you value control vs. speed?
- Do you already know how to code?

For this book, we'll build with code — it gives you the most control and the most transferable skills. But don't be a snob about no-code tools. Use the right tool for the job.

> ⚠️ **Watch Out!**
> No-code vendor lock-in is real. When you build in Bubble, your data and logic live in Bubble's proprietary system. If Bubble raises prices, gets acquired, or shuts down, you have a problem. For side projects this is fine. For a serious business, think carefully about how dependent you want to be on a single platform.

---

## When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Approach |
|---|---|
| Testing an idea in an afternoon | Bubble, Glide, or Zapier |
| Personal workflow automation | Zapier, Make, or n8n |
| Internal business tool | Retool, Softr, or Bubble |
| AI prototype for user testing | Lovable or Bolt.new |
| Production app with custom behavior | Code (Parts 3 and 4 of this book) |
| Complex AI pipeline prototype | Flowise or Langflow |

> 🔨 **Try It Yourself**
> Go to make.com (free account) and build a simple automation: connect it to a Google Sheet and have it send each new row's content to OpenAI for a one-line summary, then write that summary back to the sheet. This takes about 30 minutes and teaches you more about AI API calls than reading this chapter twice.

---

## Summary

- No-code tools are genuinely useful — not just training wheels
- Workflow tools (Zapier, Make, n8n): best for automations and integrating AI into existing processes
- App builders (Bubble, Glide): best for MVPs and simple apps
- AI code generators (Lovable, Bolt): best for fast prototyping, ships real code
- AI pipeline builders (Flowise, Langflow): best for experimenting with AI architectures
- Use the right tool for the job; don't be dogmatic about "real code only"
